turns.content:
the spec entry structure and contents are badly structured and have unnecessary workflow instructions. the current guidance policy for specifying target behavior for components is in bad shape. We need a complete overhaul.

The specification database and spec-api should be used to anchor the desired behavior of the system and ground it with entity links and background knowledge, aswell as provide housekeeping facilities.

Agents should describe new features as sets of required validation setps to pass in as many formats as possible (triangulation by using executable tests, formal specification in natural language and code schema references).

A spec should encapsulate:
- the short story of the behavior
- the provided surface contracts which consumers of the behavior can rely on
- executable tests to run to prove the contracts are always satisfied
- related implementation tickets

specs do not not need to include full expanded entity contents, they can rely on entity references and context rendering to expand entities to the appropriate level of detail at the latest database state.

Review all of the guidance files and overhaul it completely. use tickets to plan your work

I’m starting by anchoring this in the existing workflow surfaces: I’ll check the ticket board, inspect the guidance files that currently govern specs and agent workflow, and open a planning ticket before any edits.

arguments.content:
# Objective

Overhaul the repository guidance for describing target behavior so specs become concise behavior contracts anchored by the spec store, entity references, validation triangulation, and related implementation tickets.

# Why

The current spec entry shape and the surrounding agent guidance still mix behavior contracts with workflow narration, rollout prose, and housekeeping instructions. That makes specs noisy, inconsistent across prompts, and weak as the canonical description of what a component promises.

# Scope

- rewrite the canonical spec contract for component behavior around a short behavior story, provided surface contracts, executable validation, and related tickets
- remove unnecessary workflow prose from the spec contract and move ticket/process guidance to the right workflow surfaces
- align AGENTS, spec instructions, prompts, and the Spec Agent so they all describe the same behavior-first contract
- make validation triangulation explicit: executable tests, natural-language contract text, and code/schema references where available
- preserve entity-link based background knowledge instead of duplicating expanded entity contents inside specs

# Acceptance Criteria

- the owning spec entry defines specs as behavior contracts anchored in the spec database rather than workflow-heavy documents
- AGENTS plus the relevant spec/ticket prompts and agent guidance all converge on the same spec shape and responsibilities
- the guidance tells agents to describe new features as required validation steps across executable, natural-language, and code/schema forms when available
- the guidance explicitly treats full entity expansion as optional and prefers references plus context rendering
- focused validation demonstrates the updated guidance is structurally sound

# Validation

- focused reads/diff review of the rewritten guidance surfaces
- spec health for the updated canonical spec
- targeted file diagnostics for touched markdown/guidance files

fields: title=Behavior-first specification contract and validation triangulation

expected_properties.statement: The spec store anchors desired component behavior as a compact contract instead of a workflow-heavy narrative.
Each spec states the provided surface contracts that consumers may rely on.
New behavior is described through required validation steps in multiple compatible forms: executable checks, natural-language contract clauses, and code or schema references when available.
Specs prefer entity references and context rendering over embedding fully expanded entity contents.
CLI, MCP, HTTP, and viewer surfaces render or expand the same stored contract instead of redefining it.

evidence_requirements.description: The canonical spec body and the guidance surfaces describe the same behavior-first spec contract and responsibilities.
Focused validation confirms the updated spec entry and guidance surfaces remain structurally sound after the overhaul.

acceptance_criteria.statement: The canonical contract defines a spec in terms of a short behavior story, provided surface contracts, executable validation requirements, and related implementation tickets.
Workflow instructions, rollout notes, blockers, and housekeeping guidance are kept out of the spec contract itself unless they directly change the promised behavior.
Guidance for new features requires validation triangulation across executable checks, natural-language contract text, and code or schema references when those references exist.
Specs rely on entity references and context rendering for expandable background knowledge instead of duplicating full entity payloads in the spec body.
All tool surfaces treat the stored spec as the canonical contract and only adapt or render it for their transport.

arguments.body:
# Summary

Define the repository's default specification contract around the behavior a component promises, not around workflow narration. A spec is the compact system anchor for intended behavior, consumer-visible contracts, and the validation required to keep those contracts true.

## Required Shape

Each spec encapsulates:

- the short story of the behavior
- the provided surface contracts that consumers can rely on
- the required validation steps and executable tests or checks that prove those contracts
- the related implementation tickets

Specs may link entities and background knowledge by reference. They do not need to inline fully expanded entity contents when context rendering can resolve the latest database state.

## Authoring Rules

- Keep rollout plans, migration notes, blockers, operator checklists, and housekeeping instructions in tickets or workflow guidance unless they directly change the promised behavior.
- When describing a new feature, triangulate the target behavior in as many compatible forms as possible:
  - executable validation steps and commands
  - natural-language contract clauses
  - code, schema, API, or other structural references when available
- Keep the spec short enough to review as a contract. Link outward for implementation detail, evidence history, and background knowledge.

## Provided Contracts

- Consumers can treat the spec store as the canonical anchor for desired behavior.
- CLI, MCP, HTTP, and viewer surfaces may render or expand the contract, but they do not redefine it.
- Entity references plus the latest database state are authoritative for expanding supporting detail.

## Validation Expectations

- Every contract clause should map to at least one required validation step when feasible.
- Validation may mix executable tests, focused manual checks, and schema or code reference checks, but the spec must say what proves the contract.
- Missing or failing evidence should be representable without rewriting the contract itself.

## Related Work

- Related implementation and follow-up work lives in linked tickets.
- Housekeeping facilities belong to the spec system and validation stores, not to the narrative content of each spec.

turns.content: I’ve finished the source-of-truth spec rewrite. The file edits now are purely alignment work: AGENTS, the spec instruction, and the ticket/spec prompts will all be rewritten to mirror that contract and to move workflow prose back out of specs.

arguments.explanation: Align repository guidance files with the new behavior-first spec contract and validation triangulation policy.
arguments.input:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: c:\Users\linus\git\graph_app\context-engine\AGENTS.md
@@
 ## Operating Principles
 
 - Gather context before coding. Do not guess.
 - Read existing tests to infer expected behavior.
 - For implementation work, create or update the relevant ticket(s) before editing code.
 - For new or changed requirements and goals, create or update the relevant spec before implementation proceeds.
+- Treat specs as behavior-first contracts: record the short behavior story, the provided surface contracts, the required validation, and the related tickets.
+- Keep rollout notes, blockers, housekeeping, and other workflow prose in tickets or workflow guidance, and prefer entity references over duplicating fully expanded background content inside specs.
 - Keep the ticket, spec, validation, and documentation trail current so review and status summaries stay accurate.
 - Prefer bash commands over PowerShell/cmd.
 - Use Unix-style paths (`/`) in commands and docs.
 - Read test logs in `target/test-logs/` for debugging instead of relying on truncated test stdout.
@@
 ## Task Routing
 
 - Any requested implementation or behavior change: create or update the tracking ticket(s) first, then create or update the relevant spec before editing files.
+- When a spec changes, anchor it in the spec store as a behavior contract and describe the required validation in as many compatible forms as possible: executable checks, natural-language clauses, and code or schema references when available.
 - Simple fix (1-2 files): after the ticket/spec setup when requirements or behavior change, gather context, implement, validate, update docs, verify spec links, and move the ticket to `in-review`.
 - Bug fix: after the ticket/spec setup, follow `.agents/prompts/debug-test.prompt.md` when available.
 - Feature or refactor (>5 files, >100 LOC, or unclear scope): use `.agents/prompts/tickets.prompt.md` to establish the ticket set, then `.agents/prompts/spec.prompt.md` to update the spec before implementation.
 - Unfamiliar module or unclear behavior: follow `.agents/prompts/research.prompt.md` when available before locking the spec or implementation plan.
 - Swarm execution: use `.agents/prompts/swarm-worker.prompt.md`.
*** Update File: c:\Users\linus\git\graph_app\context-engine\.agents\instructions\spec-system.instructions.md
@@
----
-description: "Use when creating or updating specs. Covers spec discovery, authoring workflow, and traceability expectations across tickets, tests, validation, and related specs."
-applyTo: ".spec/**,**/.spec/**,memory-viewers/spec-viewer/**,memory-api/crates/spec-api/**,memory-api/tools/cli/spec-cli/**,memory-api/tools/mcp/spec-mcp/**"
----
-
-## Scope
-
-Applies when creating, updating, reviewing, or validating specifications through the repository's spec system.
-
-## Design Constraints
-
-- Prefer one clear owning spec per behavior or requirement slice.
-- Keep specs focused on system properties, acceptance criteria, evidence, and non-goals.
-- Keep implementation plans, rollout sequencing, and execution notes in tickets unless they materially affect the contract.
-- Preserve traceability between specs, tickets, validation evidence, and neighboring specs.
-
-## Spec Quality — Standing Obligations
-
-These rules apply whenever spec work is involved, not only when editing spec-system code.
-
-### Orientation (start of every session)
-
-Before writing or editing a spec:
-
-- search existing specs for the behavior first
-- search related tickets so the spec can link the current execution plan
-- check whether a neighboring or parent spec already owns the requested slice
-
-Prefer `spec-mcp` and `ticket-mcp` tools when available. Fall back to `./target/debug/spec.exe` and `./target/debug/ticket.exe` when needed.
-
-### Discovery Before Creating
-
-Always search for an existing spec before creating a new one. Duplicate specs weaken the repository contract.
-
-Prefer updating a matching spec when:
-- the behavior belongs to the same component and scope
-- the existing spec can absorb the acceptance criteria without becoming unfocused
-- the requested change is a refinement rather than a new contract slice
-
-Create a new spec when:
-- the requested behavior is a distinct contract slice
-- the existing spec would become too broad or mix unrelated concerns
-- the new work needs its own acceptance criteria and evidence trail
-
-## Spec Authoring Workflow
-
-### Choose Component, Slug, and Parent
-
-- Use the owning subsystem or workflow area as the component.
-- Keep slugs lowercase, use `-` within segments, and `/` between segments.
-- Prefer a parent spec only when the new slice is clearly a child of an existing broader contract.
-- Avoid creating shallow duplicate siblings with overlapping goals.
-
-### Structure the Spec
-
-Each spec should make review straightforward. Capture, when relevant:
-- goal or intended behavior
-- problem or current-state gap
-- scope
-- non-goals
-- acceptance criteria
-- traceability or evidence expectations
-
-Acceptance criteria should be concrete enough that a reviewer can tell what evidence proves the work is done.
-
-### Link Tickets, Tests, and Related Specs
-
-Specs should explicitly link the work needed to satisfy or verify the contract.
-
-- Link the exact related ticket folder paths returned by ticket tools. Do not synthesize ticket paths.
-- Record the validation plan or completed validation results needed to evaluate the spec.
-- Link related specs when they define prerequisites, shared contracts, or adjacent behavior.
-- When docs or generated guidance are part of the deliverable, include them in the traceability or evidence section.
-
-Use a clear evidence vocabulary when possible, including validation commands, expected evidence objects, and blocked or passing results.
-
-### Validation Before Review
-
-Before moving spec work toward review, verify:
-- the acceptance criteria are testable
-- the linked tickets are sufficient to execute the work
-- the validation evidence is concrete, not implied
-- related specs are linked where cross-spec behavior matters
-- the spec still describes the contract, not a ticket-sized implementation plan
-
-## Workflow Expectations
-
-- Instruction precedence and exception handling follow the AGENTS matrix at `agent-rules/instruction-precedence-and-exceptions`.
-- For files matched by this instruction `applyTo`, this file is the path-scoped source of truth for spec workflows.
-- If this file conflicts with AGENTS global defaults, apply this file for spec-scoped work and keep AGENTS as the default elsewhere.
-- If two spec instructions conflict at the same specificity, prefer the newer explicitly scoped rule and record the rationale in ticket/spec notes.
-- If tooling or platform constraints prevent strict compliance, apply the safest feasible behavior and record the limitation in validation evidence.
-
-- When requirements, goals, or behavior change, create or update the relevant spec before implementation.
-- When implementation reveals a contract change, update the spec and its evidence trail immediately.
-- Keep ticket links, validation results, and related spec references current enough that another agent can continue the work without reconstructing intent.
-- Use the Spec Agent when work is primarily about creating or refining specs rather than implementing code.
-- If ambiguity remains after focused search, ask one concise clarification instead of guessing.
+---
+description: "Use when creating or updating specs. Defines the repository's behavior-first spec contract, validation triangulation, and traceability expectations."
+applyTo: ".spec/**,**/.spec/**,memory-viewers/spec-viewer/**,memory-api/crates/spec-api/**,memory-api/tools/cli/spec-cli/**,memory-api/tools/mcp/spec-mcp/**"
+---
+
+## Scope
+
+Applies when creating, updating, reviewing, or validating specifications through the repository's spec system.
+
+## Core Contract
+
+- A spec is the canonical store-backed anchor for desired behavior.
+- Prefer one clear owning spec per behavior slice.
+- Each spec should stay compact and reviewable.
+- Put workflow narration, rollout plans, blockers, and housekeeping in tickets or workflow guidance unless they directly change the promised behavior.
+- Prefer entity references and context rendering over copying fully expanded entity contents into the spec body.
+
+## Required Shape
+
+Each spec should encapsulate these four things:
+
+- the short story of the behavior
+- the provided surface contracts that consumers can rely on
+- the validation required to prove those contracts stay true
+- the related implementation tickets
+
+When helpful, add neighboring specs, non-goals, or evidence notes, but do not let them crowd out the contract.
+
+## Validation Triangulation
+
+When a spec describes new or changed behavior, capture the required validation in as many compatible forms as possible:
+
+- executable tests or commands
+- natural-language contract clauses
+- code, schema, API, or other structural references when available
+
+The goal is not verbosity. The goal is to make the contract provable from more than one direction.
+
+## Discovery Before Creating
+
+Before writing or editing a spec:
+
+- search existing specs for the owning behavior
+- search related tickets so the spec can link the active implementation work
+- check whether a parent or neighboring spec already owns the contract slice
+
+Prefer `spec-mcp` and `ticket-mcp` tools when available. Fall back to `./target/debug/spec.exe` and `./target/debug/ticket.exe` when needed.
+
+Prefer updating a matching spec when:
+
+- the behavior belongs to the same component and scope
+- the existing spec can absorb the contract without becoming unfocused
+- the requested change refines an existing promise rather than introducing a new one
+
+Create a new spec when:
+
+- the behavior is a distinct contract slice
+- the existing spec would mix unrelated promises
+- the new work needs its own validation and ticket traceability
+
+## Authoring Rules
+
+- Use the owning subsystem or workflow area as the component.
+- Keep slugs lowercase, use `-` within segments, and `/` between segments.
+- Prefer a parent spec only when the new slice is clearly subordinate to a broader contract.
+- Link the exact related ticket folder paths returned by ticket tools. Do not synthesize ticket paths.
+- Link related specs when they define prerequisites, shared contracts, or adjacent behavior.
+- Record the validation plan or completed validation results needed to evaluate the contract.
+
+## Anti-Patterns
+
+Do not treat these as required spec sections unless they directly change the promised behavior:
+
+- implementation plans
+- rollout sequencing
+- migration journals
+- status updates
+- copied entity dumps
+- operator housekeeping checklists
+
+## Validation Before Review
+
+Before moving spec work toward review, verify:
+
+- the behavior story and consumer contracts are explicit
+- the validation is concrete enough to prove or falsify the contract
+- the linked tickets are sufficient to execute the work
+- the spec still reads like a contract, not a worklog
+
+## Workflow Expectations
+
+- Instruction precedence and exception handling follow the AGENTS matrix at `agent-rules/instruction-precedence-and-exceptions`.
+- For files matched by this instruction `applyTo`, this file is the path-scoped source of truth for spec workflows.
+- When requirements, goals, or behavior change, create or update the relevant spec before implementation.
+- When implementation reveals a contract change, update the spec and its evidence trail immediately.
+- Keep ticket links, validation results, and related spec references current enough that another agent can continue the work without reconstructing intent.
+- Use the Spec Agent when work is primarily about creating or refining specs rather than implementing code.
+- If ambiguity remains after focused search, ask one concise clarification instead of guessing.

 1. Treat the text typed after `/spec` as the source request.

 5. If `spec-mcp` is unavailable, fall back to `./target/debug/spec.exe` and register `.spec/specs` with `spec.exe add-root .spec/specs --label default --json` if needed.
 6. Infer a clear title, slug, component, and parent. Keep slugs lowercase, use `-` within segments, and `/` between segments.
-7. Prefer updating a matching spec over creating a near-duplicate. If no matching spec exists, create one in `draft` state.
-8. Ensure the spec body captures the intended system properties, explicit acceptance criteria, required evidence or traceability needed to evaluate implementation, and non-goals when obvious. +7. Prefer updating a matching spec over creating a near-duplicate. If no matching spec exists, create one in `draft` state.
+8. Ensure the spec body captures the short behavior story, the provided surface contracts, the required validation, and the related implementation tickets.
+9. Describe required validation in as many compatible forms as possible: executable checks, natural-language contract clauses, and code or schema references when available.
+10. Use entity references and context rendering for expandable background knowledge instead of duplicating fully expanded entity contents in the spec body.
+11. Keep problem statements, rollout sequencing, blockers, migration notes, and implementation details in related tickets unless they directly change the promised behavior.

Use the exact returned folder path as the link base and append `/ticket.toml` for the markdown target so editors can open the file directly.

 ## Required Workflow
 
 1. Anchor on the requested behavior, affected feature, or existing spec.
 2. Search existing specs first, then related tickets, to avoid duplicates and identify the owning component and parent.
 3. Decide whether to update an existing spec or create a draft spec with a clear slug and parent.
 4. Write or refine the spec so it captures:
-   - goal or intended behavior
-   - scope and non-goals
-   - explicit acceptance criteria
-   - required traceability and evidence
+   - the short story of the behavior
+   - the provided surface contracts consumers can rely on
+   - the validation required to prove those contracts
+   - the related implementation tickets
+5. Triangulate the validation in as many compatible forms as possible:
+   - executable tests or commands
+   - natural-language contract clauses
+   - code, schema, or API references when available
 5. Link the spec to:
    - exact related ticket folder paths returned by ticket tools
    - validation commands, planned evidence, or completed results

-6. Before finishing, verify the spec is reviewable: the acceptance criteria are testable, the evidence plan is concrete, and the linked tickets/specs are sufficient for implementation follow-through.
-7. Recommend the next workflow step: create tickets, update tickets, implement, or validate.
+6. Before finishing, verify the spec is reviewable: the behavior contract is explicit, the validation is concrete, and the linked tickets/specs are sufficient for implementation follow-through.
+7. Recommend the next workflow step: create tickets, update tickets, implement, or validate.
@@

The spec contract is now anchored as a behavior-first definition in [.spec/specs/298f2f92-df7e-4c8a-b4fe-b63c27622142/body.md](.spec/specs/298f2f92-df7e-4c8a-b4fe-b63c27622142/body.md#L3) and in the structured store fields at [.spec/specs/298f2f92-df7e-4c8a-b4fe-b63c27622142/spec.toml](.spec/specs/298f2f92-df7e-4c8a-b4fe-b63c27622142/spec.toml#L5). It now defines a spec as: short behavior story, provided surface contracts, required validation, and related implementation tickets, with explicit validation triangulation and reference-first entity expansion.

I aligned the guidance chain that actually propagates this policy: [AGENTS.md](AGENTS.md#L15) and [AGENTS.md](AGENTS.md#L51), [.agents/instructions/spec-system.instructions.md](.agents/instructions/spec-system.instructions.md#L18), [.agents/prompts/spec.prompt.md](.agents/prompts/spec.prompt.md#L22), [.agents/prompts/tickets.prompt.md](.agents/prompts/tickets.prompt.md#L31), [.agents/prompts/ticket.prompt.md](.agents/prompts/ticket.prompt.md#L30), and the Spec Agent at [.agents/agents/spec.agent.md](.agents/agents/spec.agent.md#L39). I also updated the downstream planning/validation prompts to carry the same contract and structural-reference language at [.agents/prompts/user-training.prompt.md](.agents/prompts/user-training.prompt.md#L22), [.agents/prompts/tdd.prompt.md](.agents/prompts/tdd.prompt.md#L32), and [.agents/prompts/ticket-next.prompt.md](.agents/prompts/ticket-next.prompt.md#L35).

Validation was limited to the relevant surfaces for this documentation/spec-store change: `get_errors` reported no issues on the touched guidance files, and `spec_health` passed cleanly for spec `298f2f92-df7e-4c8a-b4fe-b63c27622142` after adding fulfillment summaries. The tracking ticket is [.ticket/tickets/37d7fac3-cc7d-44b9-b6e1-f199fca8e901/ticket.toml](.ticket/tickets/37d7fac3-cc7d-44b9-b6e1-f199fca8e901/ticket.toml); it is now in `in-review`, and the board claim was checked out.

arguments.command: rtk ./target/debug/ticket.exe search --workspace . "migrate all specs behavior-first structure" --toon
arguments.explanation: Search for an existing ticket covering full spec-structure migration to avoid duplicate planning.

arguments.content:
# Objective

Migrate all existing specs in the workspace store to the aligned behavior-first structure.

# Scope

- migrate every spec body to the aligned contract shape
- ensure each migrated spec captures behavior story, provided contracts, required validation, and related implementation tickets
- preserve source intent by moving legacy rollout/worklog prose into compact background sections or linked ticket references
- keep entity details reference-first (no mandatory expanded payload duplication)
- run spec health validation across the full store after migration

# Acceptance Criteria

- all specs in `.spec/specs/` have the aligned top-level structure
- migrated specs explicitly include executable/operational validation steps and natural-language contract clauses
- code/schema/API references are included when available from existing content
- no spec health errors remain for migrated entries
- migration changes are reviewable and traceable to this ticket

# Validation

- full store spec health check
- targeted diffs for representative migrated specs
- no markdown diagnostics in touched files

turns.content:
### Overview
Resume the behavior-first spec-contract track in [c:/Users/linus/git/graph_app/context-engine](.). This track already completed two major slices: guidance overhaul and full spec-store migration. Current objective is to package and land the work cleanly (reviewable commit set, no session-noise artifacts), while preserving validated outcomes: all specs aligned and health-clean.

### Long-Horizon Goal
Ship a stable, behavior-first spec system where specs are concise contracts (behavior story, surface contracts, required validation, linked implementation tickets), with migration applied across all existing specs and guidance surfaces aligned.

### Findings
- Canonical contract and guidance are already rewritten and tracked in in-review ticket [37d7fac3](.ticket/tickets/37d7fac3-cc7d-44b9-b6e1-f199fca8e901/ticket.toml).
- Full migration was executed and tracked in in-review ticket [38bbb8e2](.ticket/tickets/38bbb8e2-19d1-4522-8577-dd922e70d6b7/ticket.toml).
- Spec-store migration coverage is complete: 190/190 spec bodies carry aligned marker.
- Global spec health is clean: 190 specs checked, 0 issues.
- Board is currently clear (no active entries), so restart can check in immediately.

### Decisions That Matter
- The behavioral source of truth is the canonical spec contract at [298f2f92 spec](.spec/specs/298f2f92-df7e-4c8a-b4fe-b63c27622142/spec.toml) with body in [298f2f92 body](.spec/specs/298f2f92-df7e-4c8a-b4fe-b63c27622142/body.md).
- Migration preserved legacy content under structured aligned headers rather than deleting prior prose.
- Validation triangulation is mandatory in guidance: executable checks + natural-language clauses + code/schema/API refs when available.
- Entity-reference-first behavior was adopted (avoid bloating specs with fully expanded context payloads).

### Blockers And Risks
- No hard technical blocker on the spec track.
- Working tree is very dirty with many unrelated/session-generated files; commit hygiene is the main risk.
- Session-capture artifacts under [.session](.session) are present and can pollute commits if not excluded intentionally.

### Key Entities
- [37d7fac3 ticket](.ticket/tickets/37d7fac3-cc7d-44b9-b6e1-f199fca8e901/description.md): guidance overhaul scope and acceptance criteria.
- [38bbb8e2 ticket](.ticket/tickets/38bbb8e2-19d1-4522-8577-dd922e70d6b7/description.md): migration scope and acceptance criteria.
- [canonical spec contract](.spec/specs/298f2f92-df7e-4c8a-b4fe-b63c27622142/body.md): defines aligned structure semantics.
- [spec-system instructions](.agents/instructions/spec-system.instructions.md): path-scoped behavior-first contract guidance.
- [AGENTS](AGENTS.md): operating rules, quality gates, and precedence policy.
- [SESSION_CONTEXT_POLICY](SESSION_CONTEXT_POLICY.md): compact context/handoff policy to avoid transcript-noise replay.
- [ticket-next prompt](.agents/prompts/ticket-next.prompt.md): preferred next-ticket execution pattern.
- [next prompt](.agents/prompts/next.prompt.md): continuity workflow for restart sequencing.
- [ticket system instructions](.agents/instructions/ticket-system.instructions.md): ticket lifecycle and board semantics.
- [ticket CLI docs](memory-api/tools/cli/ticket-cli/README.md): authoritative CLI syntax for board/ticket operations.
- [ticket MCP docs](memory-api/tools/mcp/ticket-mcp/README.md): MCP equivalents for ticket operations.
- [spec CLI docs](memory-api/tools/cli/spec-cli/README.md): spec operations and validation workflow.
- [migration script](target/tmp/migrate_specs_aligned.py): migration logic used for bulk rewrite.
- [session capture pointer 1](.session/sessions/bd055707-d03f-4fb6-9862-d8ed54e94caa/transcript.json): prior ticket/instruction-governance context, only if forensic detail is needed.
- [session capture pointer 2](.session/sessions/d6510f8c-02b3-432a-9c21-836ba993a5fa/transcript.json): broader planning run, only for audit replay.

### Next Actions (Execution Order)
1. Check in on ticket [38bbb8e2](.ticket/tickets/38bbb8e2-19d1-4522-8577-dd922e70d6b7/ticket.toml) with file ownership limited to spec/guidance files intended for this track.
2. Produce a strict include list for this track (spec/guidance/ticket artifacts) and explicitly exclude session captures under [.session](.session) unless intentionally required.
3. Re-run the two key validations as restart proof:
   - coverage marker count across spec bodies
   - full spec health check
4. Review staged diff for only this track’s files; ensure no unrelated ticket tracks are included.
5. Move both tickets to done only after commit-ready evidence is captured and linked in ticket descriptions.

### Definition Of Done
